There's a lot more to a home than what you can see in photos — and now there's a way to measure it.

Every single-family home in America now has a Pearl SCORE™ — a rating from 1 to 1,000 that measures five things that shape daily life: safety, comfort, operations, resilience, and energy.
Think of it like a report card for a house. It takes all the technical stuff — insulation ratings, HVAC efficiency, air quality — and translates it into clear answers about what a home costs to run, how comfortable it is, and how well it holds up over time.

For homeowners, it means you can claim your home's profile and update it for free. If you've invested in things like new windows, better insulation, solar panels, or a smart thermostat, you can make sure that value is captured and visible — so when it comes time to sell, you get credit for the upgrades you've made.

For real estate agents, Pearl SCORE™ offers a standardized way to talk about home performance with clients — no building science degree required. It's one more tool to help guide buyers and showcase the full value of a seller's home.

Two homes. Same price. Same neighborhood. Which one is right for you?

One might have lower monthly operating costs, better insulation, and rooms that stay comfortable year-round. The other might look stunning but come with higher bills and systems nearing the end of their life.

Pearl SCORE™ helps you see the difference—measuring how well homes perform across the five critical performance pillars: Safety, Comfort, Operations, Resilience, and Energy. It's the difference between how a home looks versus what it's like to live in every day. 

The best part? Homes that have been claimed and verified by their owners have even more detailed data. That means sellers who've invested in their home can make sure you see what they've done.

We're still processing all of the incredible take-aways from the biggest conference in real estate, and one thing was clear: AI is everywhere in the industry right now.

But here's the encouraging part—the agents getting the best results aren't the ones automating everything. They're the ones using technology to support what already makes them great: genuine conversations, authentic relationships, and real expertise.

#InmanConnect #RealEstate

One thing was crystal clear at Inman Connect NYC: AI has arrived in real estate. It was on every stage, in every hallway conversation, and in every booth on the expo floor.

But here's what actually stood out: it wasn't the flashiest tool. It was authenticity.

In a sea of AI-generated listing descriptions, AI-written emails, and AI-powered chatbots, the agents who are winning are the ones whose marketing still feels human. Real voices. Real stories. Real engagement with clients—especially in those early moments when trust is being built.

AI is a powerful tool. But the agents getting the most from it aren't using it to replace their personality—they're using it to amplify what's already authentic about how they serve clients. The difference is noticeable, and buyers can tell.

The takeaway for agents: lean into AI where it saves you time and scales your work. But protect the things that make you... you. That's what clients remember, refer, and come back to.

#InmanConnect #ICNY #RealEstate #AI #Authenticity #HomePerformance

Buyer priorities shift—and agents who spot the shifts early serve their clients best.

A decade ago, everyone wanted open floor plans. Before that, granite countertops. The market evolves based on how people actually live. Something different is happening now: the shift isn't about aesthetics. It's about function. And the forces driving it are only gaining momentum.

Four forces are converging: affordability concerns making operating costs more relevant to every transaction, the remote work effect deepening what buyers notice and expect from their homes, growing awareness of regional resilience needs, and a generation of buyers who expect data transparency as a baseline.

The question "how well does this house work?" is joining "what's the school district?" And agents who can confidently answer that question are already differentiating themselves—adding real value, building deeper trust, and helping clients make decisions they feel great about.

The tools to have those conversations are available now.

The best agents aren't chasing flashy AI tools. They're focused on authenticity—real relationships, genuine conversations, and actually educating the people they serve.

The industry is also buzzing about a major shift in how homes are listed and discovered. We believe in more transparency, not less. Buyers deserve to know how a home performs—not just what it looks like.

#InmanConnect #RealEstate #HomePerformance

The team was at Inman Connect New York last week, and our heads are still spinning—in the best way.

Three big takeaways from three packed days in Manhattan:

1. AI is everywhere, all the time. Every stage, every hallway conversation, every booth. But what stood out in the noise wasn't the flashiest AI tool—it was authenticity. In a sea of AI-generated content, what cuts through is real marketing and genuine early client engagement. The agents winning aren't automating their personalities away. They're using technology to amplify what's already authentic about how they serve clients.

2. The smartest agents are investing in their past clients. The most successful agents at Inman shared a common thread: they're spending the majority of their marketing budget on people who already know, like, and trust them. Not cold leads. Not mass advertising. Past clients—with a focus on adding value through education, not just staying "top of mind."

3. The private listings battle isn't going away. In fact, what we're hearing suggests this may be the early innings of a seismic shift in the MLS landscape. The debate around data access is intensifying—and it's going to reshape how homes are marketed and discovered. This is one to watch closely.

Grateful to have been in the room for these conversations. More to come on what it all means for home performance—and for the agents leading the way.
#InmanConnect #ICNY #RealEstate #AI #PrivateListings #HomePerformance

As we head to Inman Connect in NYC today, we're thrilled to share our official press release: Pearl has been named the winner of Inman's 2025 Best of Proptech Award in the Sustainability & Climate Resilience category. 🏆

It's an honor to be recognized among so many innovators reshaping the future of residential real estate. As Inman put it, the award honors companies "at the forefront of property technology innovation who are fundamentally reshaping how homes are built, financed, bought, sold, and managed."

For decades, real estate transactions have focused on price, location, and aesthetics. But homebuyers have only had half the story. What about how a home actually performs? How well does it protect the the health of its occupants? Does it maintain comfortable temperatures year-round? Are the utility costs consistent? How well can it withstand environmental stress? Does it generate, store, and manage its own energy?

Pearl is changing that. With the Pearl Home Performance Registry™ and Pearl SCORE™, we're making home performance measurable, searchable, and actionable—so buyers can make confident decisions with fewer surprises.

Today's first-time homebuyers are 40 years old on average. And they're typically purchasing homes built around 1980—making buyer and property roughly the same age.

That's not just a fun stat. It's a market reality with real implications.

These homes were built before modern energy codes, before we understood indoor air quality, and before climate resilience entered the conversation. More than half of America's housing stock was built before 1980. This is what's available—especially for first-time buyers entering the market at a stage of life when every dollar matters.

Buyers are asking harder questions: What will my utility bills actually be? Is the air safe for my family? Can this home handle severe weather? Traditional listings don't answer those questions.

As the average first-time homebuyer reaches 40 and confronts properties of equal vintage, the conversation must shift from what homes look like to how they perform. In a market where aging housing stock meets increasingly informed buyers, transparency isn’t just valuable — it’s essential.

Read the full article from Cynthia Adams on Agent Publishing LLC → https://lnkd.in/erXMd8cm
